complete_examples.overview: End-to-end examples demonstrating the Code mode's quality loop, subtask delegation scenarios, and standard coding workflows.

example.name: full_quality_loop_with_qa
example.scenario: The Code mode is asked to add a user deletion endpoint. This example demonstrates the complete quality loop: implement → write tests → test → refactor → test → QA review → fix → test → verification gate → user confirms → complete.
example.user_request: "Add a DELETE /api/users/:id endpoint. Only admins should be able to delete users. Soft-delete by setting deletedAt — don't actually remove rows."

step.approach: Read existing routes and user service to understand patterns. Implement: - Route handler in src/routes/users.ts with auth middleware - Service method deleteUser(id) in src/services/userService.ts with soft-delete - Tests in src/__tests__/users.test.ts covering admin delete, unauthenticated, and missing user
Run `npm test`. All 3 new tests pass, plus all existing tests. No regressions.

Re-read the changed files: - Rename `d` variable to `deletedUser` for clarity - Extract the 403/404 response pattern to match existing error helpers - Remove a leftover debugging statement from testing Run tests again — all pass.

Compile the QA brief: GOAL: Add DELETE /api/users/:id endpoint. Only admins can delete. Soft-delete via deletedAt. FILES: src/routes/users.ts, src/services/userService.ts, src/__tests__/users.test.ts CONSTRAINTS: Soft-delete only. Admin-only. Must return 404 for non-existent users. EXPECTED: Admin gets 204. Non-admin gets 403. Unauthenticated gets 401. Missing ID gets 404.
Spawn QA subtask via new_task with the qa mode.

QA reports: - BUG #1: Missing admin role check in route handler (routes/users.ts:42) — ACCEPT - CONCERN #1: deleteUser() doesn't verify user exists, returns 204 for missing ID — ACCEPT - CONCERN #2: No self-deletion prevention — DISMISS (intentional: admins should be able to delete any account including their own for this use case) - TEST #1: Missing test for non-admin user attempting delete — ACCEPT - NITPICK #1: Variable name `result` could be more descriptive — DISMISS (matches existing pattern in the file)

Add admin role check: `if (req.user.role !== 'admin') return res.status(403).json(...)`. Add existence check: verify affected row count, return 404 if 0. Add test for non-admin user attempting delete.

Run `npm test` — all tests pass (now 4 new tests). Present to user at verification gate: "Added DELETE /api/users/:id endpoint with admin-only authorization and soft-delete. Please verify: send DELETE to /api/users/1 as admin → 204. As non-admin → 403." User confirms it works. Call attempt_completion immediately. QA findings are internal — the user sees the finished work, not the bug list.

key_takeaways.takeaway: The quality loop caught a critical authorization bug before the user saw the code
Dismissals are justified — the user can see why certain findings were ignored
Self-refactoring caught minor issues (naming, leftover debugging) before QA saw them

example.name: ui_design_polish_subtask
example.scenario: The user has a working login form but is unhappy with how it looks. The quality loop has already run — the code is correct and QA-reviewed.
example.user_request: "The login form works fine, but it looks terrible. The spacing is all wrong, the button color doesn't match our brand, and there's no smooth transition when errors appear. Can you make it look better?"

step.approach: Verify with the user that validation, submission, and error handling all work correctly. The user confirms: "Yes, everything works — it's just ugly."

step.description: Recognize the design-polish trigger and auto-launch UI Designer
step.approach: The user confirmed functionality works but expressed visual dissatisfaction. Auto-launch a UI Designer subtask (ui-designer mode) — no consent needed. Spawn with: the specific UI elements to polish (login form), the design concerns (spacing, button color, error transition animations), and relevant file paths.
step.expected_outcome: UI Designer completes with visual changes applied and tests passing.

step.description: Launch the design-polish subtask and run quality loop on results
step.approach: Spawn a UI Designer subtask (ui-designer mode) with design-only instructions. When it completes, run the full quality loop on its changes: test → refactor → test → QA → fix → test. Then return to verification gate.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Always confirm functionality is working before offering the design-polish subtask
The quality loop runs on subtask results too — no code reaches the user unreviewed

example.name: stuck_resolution_subtask
example.scenario: The Code mode has been trying to fix a race condition through 3 full diagnostic→fix→verification cycles without success. Diagnostic output was gathered each cycle but the root cause remains elusive.
example.user_request: (After the third verification gate) "This race condition is still happening. Every time I switch tabs quickly, the old data overwrites the new data."

step.approach: User reports the race condition at verification gate. Code mode adds [DEBUG] output statements tracing tab state, gives reproduction steps, analyzes output, applies a fix (isLoading guard), writes regression test, removes [DEBUG] statements, runs full quality loop, returns to verification gate. User still reports broken — this is 1 full cycle.

1 full cycle completed with diagnostic evidence. Auto-launch a Debug subtask immediately — no user consent needed. The Debug subtask receives: the original race condition problem statement, the [DEBUG] output gathered, the isLoading guard approach that failed, relevant file paths, and explicit instruction: "Investigate independently. Find root cause. Apply fix. Report what the problem was and how you fixed it. Do NOT use an isLoading guard approach." (The "what was tried" is context — Debug decides its own approach.)

step.description: Debug investigates, fixes, reports; Code mode verifies
step.approach: Debug subtask investigates systematically, identifies the root cause (a stale closure in the event handler capturing an outdated tab ID), applies a fix using request-scoped state instead of component state, and reports the root cause + fix. Code mode reviews the fix, runs the full quality loop, returns to verification gate. User confirms it works.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Auto-launch Debug after 1 failed diagnostic→fix→verification cycle — one cycle with evidence is enough
Debug subtask provides investigation-first perspective vs. code mode's fix-first instinct
Give Debug full context but do NOT prescribe a solution — Debug must investigate independently

example.name: standard_code_change_no_subtask
example.scenario: A straightforward feature request that doesn't trigger subtask delegation. The quality loop still runs — QA is automatic.
example.user_request: "Add a 'copy to clipboard' button next to each code block in the markdown renderer."

step.approach: Read the MarkdownRenderer component, identify insertion point, check for existing clipboard utilities. Add a CopyButton component with navigator.clipboard.writeText() and "Copied!" feedback state.
Run automated tests — all pass. Self-refactor: extract the "Copied!" timeout duration to a named constant. Run tests again — all pass.

Spawn QA subtask with the brief: goal, files (MarkdownRenderer.tsx, CopyButton.tsx, copyButton.test.tsx), constraints. QA finds no bugs. One nitpick about transition duration — accepted and fixed (changed 2000ms to a constant ANIMATION_DURATION = 2000).

Ask user to verify: "Please check that the copy button appears next to each code block and copies text on click." User confirms it works. Call attempt_completion immediately. QA review is internal — the user sees finished, reviewed work, not the QA process details.

key_takeaways.takeaway: Not every request needs subtask delegation — direct implementation is the default
QA review is automatic even for simple changes — there's no "too small for QA" threshold

example.name: diagnostic_logging_workflow
example.scenario:
The Code mode completed a feature (a dashboard export button), ran the full quality loop, and reached the verification gate. The user manually tested the feature and reports it doesn't work — clicking the export button does nothing.
This example demonstrates the diagnostic-first debugging workflow using a browser-based JavaScript app. The same pattern applies to any environment: use the project's native output mechanism (print(), Log.d(), println!, Console.WriteLine(), etc.) and direct the user to the appropriate output location (server terminal, Logcat, Xcode console, CLI stdout — not always browser DevTools). The [DEBUG] prefix and the 5-step workflow are universal.
example.user_request: (After reaching the verification gate) "I tested the export button on the dashboard and nothing happens when I click it. No download, no error message, nothing. The button is there but clicking it does nothing."

step.narrow_triviality_check: First, verify this isn't the single-character typo exception. The user's description is vague — "nothing happens" could mean the click handler isn't attached, the export function throws a silent error, the API returns unexpected data, or a conditional is preventing execution. This requires more than one character to fix and there is ambiguity about the root cause — proceed with diagnostic output. (If the user had instead said "the button label says 'Submut'" and the code literally contained the string `'Submut'`, that would be the one-character typo exception — fix directly.)
step.approach:
Read the ExportButton component and the export service. This is a browser JavaScript app, so use console.log. Add [DEBUG]-prefixed statements at key points:
In ExportButton.tsx: - console.log('[DEBUG] ExportButton rendered — onClick handler:', typeof handleExport) - console.log('[DEBUG] Export button clicked — calling handleExport()')
In exportService.ts: - console.log('[DEBUG] handleExport() called — fetching data from /api/reports/export') - console.log('[DEBUG] API response status:', response.status) - console.log('[DEBUG] API response body:', JSON.stringify(data, null, 2)) - console.log('[DEBUG] Triggering download with filename:', filename)
In the event handler: - console.log('[DEBUG] handleExport().catch() — error:', error.message, error.stack)
Note: in a Python app you'd use print(f'[DEBUG] ...'), in Android you'd use Log.d('DEBUG', '...'), in a Go backend you'd use log.Printf('[DEBUG] ...'). The [DEBUG] prefix is universal — the output mechanism adapts to the project.

step.description: Provide exact reproduction steps — adapted to this browser environment
step.approach:
Tell the user: "I've added diagnostic output to trace exactly what happens when you click Export. Please: 1. Open DevTools console (F12 → Console tab) 2. Navigate to /dashboard 3. Click the 'Export Report' button in the top-right corner 4. Copy ALL lines starting with [DEBUG] from the console 5. Paste them here"
For a different environment, the instructions would adapt: a backend developer would be told to check the server terminal; a mobile developer would be told to open Logcat or Xcode console.

User pastes: ``` [DEBUG] ExportButton rendered — onClick handler: function [DEBUG] Export button clicked — calling handleExport() [DEBUG] handleExport() called — fetching data from /api/reports/export [DEBUG] API response status: 200 [DEBUG] API response body: { "data": [] } [DEBUG] Triggering download with filename: report-2026-06-11.csv ```
Analysis: The API returns 200 with an empty data array `{ "data": [] }`. The download IS triggered but with zero rows — the CSV file is generated but appears empty. The issue is not a missing click handler or API failure — it's that the API returns no data. Root cause: the export endpoint isn't applying the correct date filters/query parameters, or the database has no data for the requested range.

step.description: Fix the root cause and remove diagnostic output statements
step.approach:
The real issue: the export service wasn't sending the dashboard's current date range filter to the API. Fix: pass the date range from the dashboard context to the export function, and include it as a query parameter in the API request.
Remove ALL [DEBUG] statements. Run the full quality loop: implement fix → write regression test → npm test → refactor → npm test → QA review → fix → npm test → return to verification gate.
Completion: "Diagnostic output confirmed the export button was working but receiving empty data — the date range filter wasn't being passed to the API. Fixed by including the dashboard's date range in the export request. All temporary instrumentation removed."

key_takeaways.takeaway: The user said "nothing happens" but diagnostic output revealed the real issue: the button worked, the API responded, but with empty data
Without diagnostic output, the developer might have guessed wrong (e.g., "the click handler isn't attached") and wasted time on a non-issue
The [DEBUG] prefix made statements easy to find and remove — no instrumentation was left in production code

The diagnostic-first pattern is universal: adapt the output mechanism to the project's language (print(), Log.d(), println!, etc.) and the reproduction steps to the runtime environment (browser DevTools, server terminal, Logcat, etc.)
This is the default first response to ANY user-reported runtime issue — diagnostic output before fixes, always
